questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 2-day history of sudden onset right-sided facial weakness. She cannot close her right eye and her smile is asymmetrical. She reports no arm or leg weakness, no speech difficulty, and no loss of hearing. On examination, there is evidence of loss of forehead wrinkling on the right side. Her ear examination is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Central facial palsy due to stroke
questions.explanation: The combination of acute onset, peripheral pattern (forehead involved), and absence of other neurological signs or ear vesicles makes Bell's palsy the most likely. Central facial palsy typically spares the forehead, while Ramsay Hunt syndrome would have vesicles in the ear or oral mucosa.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with severe left ear pain followed by left-sided facial weakness over 3 days. He has a history of hypertension but is otherwise well. On examination, there are vesicles present in the external auditory canal and on the pinna. Facial nerve function is reduced on the left side, including forehead weakness. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute otitis media with facial nerve involvement
questions.explanation: The presence of herpetic vesicles in the ear (herpes zoster oticus) with facial palsy is pathognomonic for Ramsay Hunt syndrome (varicella-zoster reactivation). Bell's palsy does not involve vesicles.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old male smoker presents with sudden onset of right-sided facial weakness and slurred speech. On examination, he has right lower motor neuron facial weakness (forehead involved) and left-sided arm and leg weakness with increased tone and hyperreflexia. There is no ear pain or vesicles. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of ipsilateral facial nerve palsy (lower motor neuron) with contralateral hemiparesis localises to the ventral pons, typical of Millard-Gubler syndrome due to brainstem stroke. Bell's palsy does not cause contralateral limb weakness.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old farmer presents with a 5-day history of headache, fever, and bilateral facial weakness. He noticed a red rash on his leg 2 weeks ago, which has resolved. He has no limb weakness or sensory loss. Examination shows bilateral lower motor neuron facial nerve palsy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilateral facial palsy is rare in Bell's palsy but common in Lyme neuroborreliosis. The history of a resolving red rash (erythema migrans) and outdoor exposure strongly points to Lyme disease.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with double vision, unsteady gait, and mild facial weakness over 3 days. She has no limb weakness. On examination, she has bilateral external ophthalmoplegia, ataxia on heel-to-toe walking, and areflexia in all four limbs. Facial nerve function is reduced bilaterally. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia is classic for Miller Fisher syndrome, a variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome. The bilateral facial weakness fits this diagnosis. Brainstem stroke would not cause areflexia or symmetrical ophthalmoplegia.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with acute right-sided facial weakness that developed over 24 hours. She cannot close her right eye or raise her eyebrow. She recently returned from a camping trip in Scotland. She has no other neurological symptoms. What is the most appropriate investigation?
questions.explanation: Lyme disease serology is indicated due to the camping history and risk of tick exposure, as Lyme disease can present with acute facial palsy. CT/MRI are not routinely recommended in typical Bell's palsy without atypical features.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old Afro-Caribbean woman presents with bilateral facial weakness over 2 weeks. She has no ear pain or vesicles. She has a dry cough and mild weight loss. What is the most appropriate investigation?
questions.explanation: Bilateral facial palsy can be due to sarcoidosis, especially in patients of Afro-Caribbean descent with respiratory symptoms. Serum ACE is a useful screening test. VZV PCR and Lyme serology are less likely without vesicles or travel history.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with her third episode of left-sided facial weakness in 2 years. Each episode has resolved over weeks. Neurological examination is normal except for left lower motor neuron facial palsy. What is the most appropriate investigation?
questions.explanation: Recurrent ipsilateral Bell's palsy raises suspicion for underlying structural causes like vestibular schwannoma or demyelination. MRI with contrast is the best test to exclude these.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with acute right facial weakness, hyperacusis, and decreased taste. MRI brain with contrast shows enhancement of the right facial nerve at the geniculate ganglion. No other lesions. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Enhancement of the facial nerve at the geniculate ganglion is a classic MRI finding in Bell's palsy. Vestibular schwannoma typically appears as a mass in the cerebellopontine angle, not linear enhancement.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old diabetic man presents with acute left facial weakness and severe ear pain. Examination reveals vesicles on the external auditory canal and pinna. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The vesicles suggest Ramsay Hunt syndrome caused by varicella zoster virus. PCR of vesicle fluid for VZV DNA is the gold standard for diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with a 1-day history of sudden onset right-sided facial weakness. She is unable to close her right eye. On examination, there is weakness of the entire right side of the face, including the forehead. No vesicles or ear pain. She is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily for 7 days
Oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days
No treatment; spontaneous recovery is expected
questions.explanation: NICE recommends oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days as first-line for Bell's palsy. Antivirals are not indicated without evidence of herpes zoster oticus, and there is no role for intramuscular steroids.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 82 mmol/mol) presents with a 2-day history of left-sided facial weakness. Examination confirms lower motor neurone facial palsy. No vesicles or ear pain. He has no other symptoms. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Surgical decompression of the facial nerve
Oral prednisolone 50 mg daily with close blood glucose monitoring
Avoid steroids due to diabetes; prescribe aciclovir instead
Oral prednisolone at a lower dose of 20 mg daily
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids are effective for Bell's palsy regardless of diabetes; blood glucose should be closely monitored and insulin may be needed. Lower doses or avoidance are not recommended, and antivirals are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with newly diagnosed Bell's palsy is being considered for oral prednisolone. She has a history of pulmonary tuberculosis but has not completed treatment. Which of the following is a contraindication to corticosteroid therapy in this patient?
questions.explanation: Systemic corticosteroids are contraindicated in the presence of untreated systemic infections such as tuberculosis, as they can worsen the infection. Hypertension, pregnancy (relative caution), hypothyroidism, and herpes simplex are not absolute contraindications.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with well-controlled HIV (CD4 >500) presents with a 3-day history of right-sided facial weakness. Examination confirms Bell's palsy. No vesicles or ear pain. What is the first-line treatment?
options: No treatment due to immunocompromised state
Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
questions.explanation: HIV infection is not a contraindication to short-course corticosteroids for Bell's palsy. First-line treatment remains oral prednisolone. Antivirals are not indicated without vesicular rash.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 1-day history of left-sided facial weakness and severe retroauricular pain. On examination, there is a lower motor neurone facial palsy with no vesicles in the ear or mouth. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Oral prednisolone plus carbamazepine for pain
Oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
questions.explanation: Severe pain is common in Bell's palsy, but without vesicles, there is no indication for antivirals. Standard treatment with prednisolone remains first-line. Carbamazepine is not indicated for Bell's palsy pain.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with acute-onset right-sided facial weakness that developed over 6 hours. She cannot close her right eye completely. There is no ear pain, rash, or hearing loss. Neurological examination reveals only ipsilateral facial weakness with intact forehead sparing? Actually forehead is involved (Bell's palsy is LMN, so forehead affected). She has no other symptoms. She is systemically well. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start oral prednisolone 50 mg daily for 10 days
Start oral aciclovir 800 mg five times daily
Prescribe artificial tears and eye ointment
Refer to neurology for nerve conduction studies
questions.explanation: Bell's palsy is diagnosed clinically. Oral prednisolone started within 72 hours improves recovery. Aciclovir is only added if Ramsay Hunt syndrome suspected (vesicles, ear pain). CT head is not indicated without atypical features (e.g., other neurology, trauma). Eye care is important but not the immediate priority; treatment to improve nerve function comes first.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 24-hour history of left-sided facial droop. He can still close his eye but with effort. There is no ear pain or rash. He has no significant past medical history. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform an ear wick test for viral PCR
questions.explanation: Within 72 hours of onset, oral prednisolone is the mainstay treatment for Bell's palsy. Aciclovir is not indicated without suspicion of Ramsay Hunt (no vesicles, pain). Ear wick test is not routine. MRI is reserved for atypical presentations or lack of recovery. Reassurance alone is insufficient as treatment improves outcome.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents with acute right-sided facial paralysis, vertigo, hearing loss on the right, and severe ear pain. There is no visible rash on the external ear. She is afebrile. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture for viral PCR
Examine the ear canal and tympanic membrane for vesicles
questions.explanation: The combination of facial palsy, vertigo, and hearing loss raises suspicion for Ramsay Hunt syndrome (varicella zoster reactivation). Even without visible rash, vesicles may be present in the ear canal (zoster sine herpete). The immediate step is to examine the ear carefully; if vesicles are found, antiviral therapy is indicated. Starting treatment without examination may be premature. CT head is not first line if no stroke features. Lumbar puncture is not needed acutely.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with Bell's palsy diagnosed 3 weeks ago presents with redness and pain in her right eye. She has been using artificial tears. On examination, there is conjunctival injection and a corneal abrasion on fluorescein staining. Her visual acuity is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Tape the eye shut at night
Arrange an EMG of the facial nerve
questions.explanation: A corneal abrasion in the setting of facial palsy requires urgent ophthalmology assessment to prevent corneal ulceration and vision loss. While eye care measures are important, specialist review is needed for definitive management (e.g., bandage contact lens, lubricants, or antibiotics). EMG is not indicated at this stage.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and atrial fibrillation on warfarin presents with sudden-onset left-sided facial weakness that developed over 1 hour. He can close his eye but with mild weakness. There is no limb weakness, dysarthria, or sensory loss. His INR is 2.5. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Given his age, vascular risk factors, and anticoagulation, the sudden onset of isolated facial palsy could be due to a lacunar stroke or intracranial haemorrhage. Urgent CT head is needed to exclude haemorrhage or infarction before any treatment. Prednisolone is not safe until a bleed is excluded. Reducing warfarin is not indicated without confirmed bleed. Blood glucose check is less urgent.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old hypertensive smoker presents with acute onset of left-sided facial weakness. On examination, he is unable to move the left side of his face. Which single finding most strongly suggests a stroke rather than Bell's palsy?
options: Loss of taste on the anterior two-thirds of the tongue
Vesicles in the external auditory canal
questions.explanation: Bell's palsy involves both upper and lower facial muscles, including the forehead. Inability to wrinkle the forehead suggests forehead sparing, which is a classic sign of stroke (upper motor neuron lesion) due to bilateral cortical innervation of the frontalis muscle.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with acute right-sided facial weakness and severe ear pain. Otoscopy reveals vesicles on the tympanic membrane and in the external auditory canal. What diagnosis is indicated by this finding?
questions.explanation: Vesicles in the ear with facial palsy is diagnostic of Ramsay Hunt syndrome due to varicella-zoster virus reactivation in the geniculate ganglion. Antiviral therapy is needed to reduce the risk of post-herpetic neuralgia and other complications.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with Bell's palsy returns complaining of eye dryness and photophobia. She cannot close her left eye completely. On slit-lamp examination with fluorescein staining, which finding would necessitate urgent ophthalmology referral?
questions.explanation: Corneal ulceration indicates exposure keratopathy from incomplete eye closure, a sight-threatening emergency. Bell's phenomenon (upward deviation of the eye) is a normal protective reflex, not a sign of harm.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old university student presents with bilateral facial weakness and tingling in his hands and feet. He also reports difficulty walking. Which single examination finding most strongly suggests a life-threatening mimic?
questions.explanation: Bilateral facial weakness with areflexia is typical of Guillain-Barre syndrome (Miller Fisher variant), which can cause respiratory muscle paralysis. Prompt recognition and admission for monitoring of respiratory function is critical.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old forestry worker presents with left-sided facial weakness of 1 day. He recalls a tick bite 3 weeks ago. Which skin finding would most strongly support a diagnosis of Lyme disease?
options: A single, expanding red patch with central clearing (erythema migrans)
questions.explanation: Erythema migrans, a target-like rash that expands over days, is the pathognomonic early sign of Lyme disease. Lyme disease can cause facial palsy and requires antibiotic treatment to prevent late complications.
